title: Roast duck with orange
subtitle: Pricked, high-roasted, with a sharp orange sauce
excerpt: A whole duck roasted at high heat with the skin pricked all over to render the fat, served with a bitter-sweet orange sauce made from the pan drippings. Duck has more fat than chicken and requires a different approach: high heat, frequent pricking, and a sauce that cuts through the richness.
sourceNotes: Duck with orange has French origins (canard à l'orange) but entered the British domestic repertoire in the 20th century. The British version is typically simpler than the French: the sauce is made from pan juices rather than a separate gastrique, and the presentation is carved rather than presented whole. The challenge is managing the fat, duck produces far more draining fat than chicken.

content.text: A standard duck weighing 2 to 2.2 kg feeds four people, though the yield of eating meat is lower than chicken of the same weight. Duck legs are meaty; the breast is modest. Expect to produce a significant quantity of rendered fat during cooking, drain the tin halfway through to avoid the duck steaming in its own fat.

Prick the skin all over with a sharp fork or skewer before roasting, this allows the fat to render out rather than pool under the skin. Internal temperature for duck: 75°C in the thigh for well-done legs, though the breast is often served slightly pink at 65°C. The leg and breast cook at different rates on a whole duck.

items.name: Orange
items.prepNote: 1 halved for the cavity, 1 juiced for the sauce

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C. Using a fork or skewer, prick the skin of the {{duck-whole}} all over, particularly the fatty areas around the thigh and breast. Season all over with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Push the halved {{orange}} into the cavity. Place breast-side up on a rack in a roasting tin. Roast for 40 minutes. Remove from the oven, carefully pour off the rendered fat from the tin (reserve it), and prick the skin again. Return to the oven for a further 40 minutes. Check the thigh temperature: 75°C for fully cooked. Rest on a warm board for 15 minutes.

While the duck rests, make the sauce. In a small pan, melt the {{granulated-sugar}} over medium heat until it forms a pale caramel. Add the {{red-wine-vinegar}} carefully (it will spit) and stir to dissolve. Add the juice of the second {{orange}} and the {{stock-chicken}}. Add 2 tablespoons of the roasting tin drippings. Simmer for 5 minutes until reduced by a third and slightly syrupy. Taste: it should be sweet, sharp, and savoury. Season with salt.

items.fix: Always roast duck on a rack. The fat must drain away from the bird. If the tin fills with fat and the rack submerges, drain it mid-cook
items.cause: Duck sitting in rendered fat rather than elevated on a rack
items.symptom: Skin not crisp, looks steamed and pale

items.fix: Take the caramel slightly darker, pale amber at minimum. The vinegar is the acid that balances the sweetness. Taste and add more vinegar in small amounts
items.cause: Sugar not caramelised enough, or insufficient vinegar

items.fix: Remove the legs and continue cooking if the breast is at the right temperature but legs are not. Or accept that on a whole bird, the breast will be medium rather than pink
items.cause: Inherent to roasting whole duck, legs always need more time
items.symptom: Breast overcooked by the time legs are done

content.text: Roast duck with orange appears at Christmas and dinner party tables more than at standard Sunday lunches. The richer meat and the sauce make it feel more celebratory than a chicken roast. The duck fat rendered during cooking is worth keeping: it makes exceptional roast potatoes and will keep in the fridge for weeks.
